text: I could help,” Newton said . “You stood and watched .” “What good could I have done, Ronin? They’d have torn me apart, and neither of us would be active now .” “It might have allowed me enough time to end them. A chance to fight. A chance…to save her .” He lifted a hand again, lightly brushing his trembling fingers over her bloody cheek. How long before her skin went cold ? “You have a chance to avenge her, hollow as that is, back in Cheyenne .” In Cheyenne. Could Ronin bring himself to go near that place again? Could he bear to walk the same paths he’d walked with Lara, to be reminded of her with each step? Back there , where — “You said you do not have the tools to save her.” Ronin looked over his shoulder, fixing an optic on Newton . “Correct.” Newton tilted his head, brow plates shifting down and drawing together. “The Air Force Base .” “ Do they ?” “They did, when I was last there. It’s been many years …” “Will she survive the journey?” The answer didn’t matter. If it was her only chance, he’d take her there, regardless. If she were to die, it would be in his arms as he attempted to save her. It would break him, all the same … “I don’t know. Time is of the essence, but she is in a delicate state. You must hurry , and — ” “ We must hurry. You’re coming .” “I-I…I can be of no — ” “You had a part in shaping this world, Newton, regardless of your intentions. You can’t hide away and pretend it doesn’t exist .” Ronin leaned forward, carefully slipping one arm beneath Lara’s knees and the other under her shoulders and neck. He lifted her off the ground and stood. Her body hung limp, broken arm folded over her midsection and the other dangling . He turned to Newton and met his optics . “This is who I am. I will not stand here and wait for her to die. Have you remembered who you are? Get me into that base, so we can save her .” Newton was silent as precious seconds ticked by. The wind sighed over the hills, carrying loose dirt that had ridden the storm. Finally, he nodded . “Let’s go, Ronin .” Chapter Twenty- Seven R onin recorded the passage of two hours, fifty-one minutes, and seventeen seconds before Newton slowed and said they were on the edge of the base. Not for the first time since meeting Lara, Ronin doubted if his internal clock was correct. Days, weeks, years had gone by as he ran, clutching Lara to him with as much delicacy and stability as he could simultaneously manage . She didn’t wake up . Despite his care, his pace — coupled with the uneven terrain — had jarred her several times, but she never stirred. It had been a struggle not to check her pulse and breathing every few dozen yards, even knowing that the slightest delay could doom her . Talkative as Newton had been in the shelter, he was silent during the trek . The wind rustled the brown, rough grass surrounding them as Newton stopped. The gentle, lazy hills were just high enough to block Cheyenne from view, but the city was so near that Ronin doubted the decision to bring her here . “Don’t leave me yet,” Ronin said to her softly. “I need you to remind me, every day.” How could he possibly carry on, taunted by his own memories of his time with her? Had she given even the smallest indication that she’d be all right — a flutter of her eyelids, a twitch of her fingers, a groan of pain — his frantic thought chains might have eased . Newton lifted an arm, extending a finger to indicate something in the field ahead. “ There .” Ronin’s optics scanned the area, seeking the entrance. He detected nothing but waving grass and small clouds of dust kicked up by the wind. On the other side of the hill lay the ruins of the base. He’d only passed through once since coming to Cheyenne, and it had supported Newton’s story — a cracked tarmac, overgrown with weeds, scorched black around the crumbled foundations of long-fallen buildings . Newton resumed his walk, and Ronin followed without comment. His experiences, as of late, had not instilled him with much trust in man or bot, but there was no other choice. Lara’s life depended on there being people here. People willing to help . They moved down a gradual slope, and partway up the next, where Newton paused to check their surroundings. Then he led Ronin into a natural depression that blocked the landscape from sight. He bent forward, brushing aside tufts of long, dry grass to reveal a metal hatch. Between the angle of the hill and the tough vegetation, it was extremely unlikely to be spotted . Newton gripped the handle and pulled the hatch open. It was surprisingly quiet, despite its apparent age . “This is the most direct route inside,” Newton said, looking up at Ronin, “without going several miles out of our way. We’ll have to take care getting her down .” Ronin stepped forward, glancing into the opening. Metal ladder rungs, set in a concrete wall, descended ten feet into a dark corridor. “They’re down there ?” “As I said, it’s been a long while since my last visit. I don’t imagine they’d have gone anywhere, but it’s entirely possible they relocated or perished in the time since .” Lara didn’t have time for Ronin to weight the options, didn’t have time for hesitation. If there was no help here, there was no help to be found at all . “Hold her. I’ll go down, and you can pass her to me,” Ronin said, turning to Newton. He ignored the blaring alarms from the
